selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 75-year-old retired woman living in Washington DC, I've seen a lot change over the years, and dealing with fake content on social media is something that weighs on my mind, especially given my tendency toward depression and high neuroticism. I use social media a few times a week, mostly to stay connected with family and friends, play video games, and share photos from my travels and hiking trips. From my perspective, managing fake content starts with a personal commitment to truth and kindness, rooted in my Christian beliefs. I believe in approaching this issue with a high level of agreeableness, as I value harmony and trust, but I've learned to be cautious. When I come across something suspicious, like a sensational news story or a too-good-to-be-true offer, I don't just share it impulsively. Instead, I take a moment to check reliable sources, often recalling lessons from my vocational diploma and my parent's mixed educational backgrounds—my father had no formal education, but my mother had a master's degree, which taught me to appreciate diverse perspectives but also seek verified information. I think it's important to talk about fake content openly with others, especially younger folks I meet through my hobbies, to foster a culture of skepticism without cynicism. Given my moderate conscientiousness, I'm not always meticulous, but I make an effort to report false posts when I can, and I limit my time online to avoid the stress that fuels my depression. Ultimately, I see managing fake content as a shared responsibility: we all need to slow down, think critically, and support each other in navigating this digital world, much like we do on a hiking trail where we watch out for each other's safety.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age (75) suggests life experience and caution; gender (woman) and sexual orientation (heterosexual) not heavily emphasized but inform general perspective; occupation (not applicable) and employment status (retired) imply free time and focus on personal interests; city (Washington DC) may indicate awareness of political fake content; education (vocational diploma) and parents' education (mixed) suggest practical, critical thinking skills; marital status (divorced) and children (0) imply independence; migration (native-born) provides cultural context; psychological feature (depressed) and neuroticism (high) lead to stress management strategies; religious beliefs (Christianity) influence ethical stance; time on social media (few times weekly) sets usage level; hobbies (videogames, travel, hiking, birdwatching) provide analogies and examples; OCEAN traits: high openness (68) supports adaptability and learning; moderate conscientiousness (38) indicates some effort but not perfectionism; moderate extraversion (52) suggests social engagement; high agreeableness (82) emphasizes harmony and trust; high neuroticism (72) drives caution and emotional response. Tone is reflective, cautious, and empathetic, avoiding bullet points for continuous prose of 250-500 words.
